description: dense commercial and entertainment district in western Tokyo
famous for Shibuya Crossing, one of the busiest intersections in the world
heavy foot traffic from tourists, students, and salarymen at all hours
a node of concentrated human emotion that makes cursed spirit activity a constant threat
Tokyo's largest commercial and administrative hub, sprawling across both east and west sides
Kabukicho on the east side is Japan's largest red-light district, dense with human vice and negative emotion
the west side hosts towering corporate skyscrapers and government offices
a district of stark contrasts — wealth and squalor, order and chaos existing within blocks of each other
one of the highest curse densities in Tokyo due to the concentration of fear, desire, and despair
youth fashion and subculture district sandwiched between Shibuya and Shinjuku
Takeshita Street is a narrow pedestrian lane packed with boutiques, crepe stands, and cosplay shops
Meiji Shrine and its surrounding forest sit at the northern edge, an island of stillness amid the noise
the contrast between sacred forest and commercial excess creates unusual cursed energy patterns
cursed spirits here tend toward the bizarre and theatrical, shaped by the district's aesthetic obsessions
upscale nightlife and arts district in central Tokyo, popular with foreign nationals and Tokyo elite
hosts some of Tokyo's most prestigious art museums and galleries alongside its nightclub strips
a district that operates on two schedules — cultured and restrained by day, hedonistic by night
the presence of old money and clan-adjacent families makes it politically sensitive territory for jujutsu operations
cursed spirits here are often older and more refined, shaped by decades of accumulated wealth and secrecy
the newest of the 23 wards, located northwest of the urban districts of Tokyo
primarily residental
contains vast green spaces, including several walkable parks
a residential and commercial district west of Shinjuku, quieter and less polished than its neighbors
Nakano Broadway is a multi-story shopping complex famous for anime, manga, and subculture merchandise
a district that attracts collectors, otaku, and people who prefer to go unnoticed
the concentration of obsession and nostalgia creates low-level but persistent cursed energy
the largest city in the Tohoku region, known as the City of Trees for its broad zelkova-lined boulevards
a regional hub with a distinct identity separate from Tokyo — proud, self-sufficient, and less densely monitored by jujutsu institutions
the distance from Tokyo's institutional oversight makes it a common location for unsanctioned sorcerer activity
the administrative and imperial heart of Tokyo, home to the Imperial Palace, the National Diet, and the Supreme Court
one of the most heavily monitored districts in Japan — police presence is constant, and unusual activity is noticed quickly
the concentration of political power makes it a pressure point for the higher-ups, who maintain strong interests in keeping it stable
cursed spirits here tend toward the ancient and territorial, having fed on centuries of political ambition and imperial authority
one of Tokyo's most affluent districts, encompassing Roppongi, Azabu, and the waterfront bay area
embassies, luxury residences, and corporate headquarters sit alongside some of the city's most expensive nightlife
old clan money has deep roots here — several established sorcerer families maintain properties in Azabu and Hiroo
the bay area at night is isolated and industrial, a sharp contrast to the manicured streets inland
known for its sword museum and festivals
traditional, old-downtown feel reminiscent of the Edo-era
slower-paced than the more urban parts of Tokyo
sumida.atmosphere: traditional melding seamlessly into the modern

historical home of jujutsu
famous for Buddhist and Shinto shrines
massive multi-level transit hub beneath Shibuya
labyrinthine underground passages connecting multiple train lines
site of significant cursed activity during peak hours

multiple entry and exit points complicating containment

meeting location for field sorcerers receiving missions
center of jujutsu life even after graduation

on a mountain on the outskirts of Tokyo

inner sanctum only open to those Tengen invites
an artificial space entirely controlled by Tengen

tunnels are dimly lit and cool

description: a major Shinto shrine dedicated to Emperor Meiji and Empress Shoken, completed in 1920
surrounded by a vast forested park of 70,000 trees, an island of silence in central Tokyo
the forest creates a natural barrier against cursed energy leakage into the surrounding district
sacred ground where the concentration of reverence and ritual suppresses weaker cursed spirits
at night the forest becomes disorienting — paths that were clear in daylight seem to shift
notable_features: the forested approach along the gravel path naturally dampens cursed energy signatures
sacred architecture creates pockets of spiritual resistance that weaker curses cannot penetrate
the contrast between sacred suppression and surrounding urban curse density attracts unusual spirit types
isolated enough at night for contained operations without civilian interference

description: Japan's largest red-light district, packed into a dense grid of narrow streets east of Shinjuku Station
host clubs, hostess bars, pachinko parlors, and yakuza-adjacent businesses operate around the clock
the concentration of desire, debt, desperation, and violence makes it one of the highest curse density zones in Tokyo
neon signage creates permanent artificial daylight while the alleys between buildings are pitch black
a place where people come to disappear — missing persons cases here are rarely investigated thoroughly
notable_features: extremely high civilian density at all hours complicates containment operations significantly
yakuza presence means non-curse related threats are a genuine operational consideration
cursed spirits here tend toward the predatory and deceptive, shaped by the district's transactional nature
multiple underground levels of bars and clubs create complex three-dimensional terrain
higher-ups have historically avoided sanctioning operations here due to exposure risk

description: the busiest train station in the world by daily passenger volume, handling over 3.5 million people per day
a subterranean labyrinth of 50 platforms, 200 exits, and multiple underground shopping concourses
the station has been expanded so many times that sections of the underground are effectively unmapped
the sheer volume of human movement generates a constant low-level cursed energy current
during rush hour the crowd pressure is physically overwhelming — ideal cover for curse activity
notable_features: the unmapped underground sections are known to harbor established cursed spirits
200 exits make perimeter containment effectively impossible during operational hours
civilian density is so high that any visible technique usage risks mass panic
the station's complexity means even experienced sorcerers can become turned around
service tunnels beneath the public concourses are accessible but legally restricted

description: isolated school on the outskirts of Kyoto
outdoor training facilities
supports more students than it has
a clean, unconspicuous office building with a single sign above the door reading 'BOTANICAL SOLUTIONS CORPORATION'
entering, the building appears deserted, with a plain, unlit hallway leading to another set of double doors, unlabelled
beyond the doors, a set of stairs lead downwards into darkness with only a set of electric lights at the top
fumbling blindly at the base of the stairs, you find a door that can only be opened with a particular application of cursed energy. You'll have to figure that out to open the door.
on the other side of the door is a humid, warm, well-lit, spacioius area reminiscent of a greenhouse or indoor botanical center
a vast upscale development combining luxury residences, corporate offices, hotels, and high-end retail
home to some of Tokyo's wealthiest residents including clan-adjacent families and political figures
the Mori Tower at its center houses executive offices and an observation deck with panoramic city views
immaculate public spaces and heavy private security create an illusion of complete safety
old money has accumulated here long enough that the cursed energy has taken on a particular character — patient, aristocratic, and territorial

notable_features: private security presence complicates covert operations significantly
several residents have known clan affiliations, making unsanctioned operations politically dangerous
the complex has its own internal security network that operates independently of police
cursed spirits here are older and more entrenched, having fed on accumulated wealth and ambition for decades
the observation deck provides unparalleled sightlines across Tokyo — tactically valuable

sparse furnishings and thin walls that carry every sound from neighboring units

thin walls mean any disturbance risks civilian attention
single entry and exit point

a balcony overlooking the street below, laundry drying in the wind

balcony provides an alternative entry and exit point
residential building means civilians on every floor
